event: Neolithic communities settle the Korean peninsula, including the northern regions, leaving comb-pattern pottery and early agricultural traces.
Traditional founding date of Gojoseon by the legendary Dangun, regarded as Korea's first kingdom and centered in the north.
Bronze Age culture spreads across the northern peninsula, with dolmen burials and the rise of walled-town polities.
Wiman seizes the throne of Gojoseon, establishing the Wiman Joseon state centered near present-day Pyongyang.
Han China conquers Wiman Joseon and establishes the Four Commanderies, including Lelang near modern Pyongyang.
The kingdom of Goguryeo is traditionally founded in the north, growing into a powerful state spanning Manchuria and the northern peninsula.
Goguryeo destroys the Lelang Commandery, ending centuries of Chinese administrative presence in the north.
Goguryeo moves its capital to Pyongyang under King Jangsu, making the city a major center of power.
Goguryeo decisively defeats a massive Sui Chinese invasion at the Battle of Salsu, securing its northern dominance.
Goguryeo falls to the allied forces of Silla and Tang China, ending the Three Kingdoms era.
Balhae is founded by Dae Joyeong in the former Goguryeo lands, ruling much of Manchuria and the northern peninsula.
Balhae collapses under the Khitan Liao dynasty, with many refugees migrating south into Goryeo territory.
Goryeo reunifies the peninsula, incorporating the northern regions and later designating Pyongyang as the Western Capital.
Goryeo defeats the Khitan at the Battle of Gwiju, securing its northern frontier.
Yi Seong-gye founds the Joseon dynasty, which rules the entire peninsula including the north for over five centuries.
Japanese forces invade during the Imjin War, ravaging the peninsula before being repelled with Ming Chinese aid.
The Later Jin (Manchu) invade Joseon through the north, forcing submission and tributary terms.
The Hong Gyeong-nae peasant rebellion erupts in the northwestern Pyongan region against Joseon discrimination and misrule.
The American merchant ship General Sherman is burned near Pyongyang after a clash with local forces.
The First Sino-Japanese War, including the Battle of Pyongyang, devastates the northern peninsula as Japan defeats China.
Japan annexes Korea, beginning a 35-year colonial occupation of the entire peninsula.
The March First Movement for independence spreads nationwide, including across the northern provinces.
Japan surrenders in World War II, and the peninsula is divided at the 38th parallel with Soviet forces occupying the north.
Soviet-backed authorities under Kim Il-sung enact sweeping land reform and nationalization in the north.
The Democratic People's Republic of Korea is proclaimed on September 9 with Kim Il-sung as premier.
North Korea invades the South on June 25, igniting the Korean War.
The Korean War ends in an armistice, leaving the peninsula divided along the Demilitarized Zone.
Kim Il-sung articulates the Juche ideology of self-reliance, which becomes the guiding state philosophy.
North Korean commandos raid the Blue House in Seoul and capture the USS Pueblo, heightening Cold War tensions.
A new socialist constitution names Kim Il-sung president and establishes Pyongyang as the capital.
North and South Korea are simultaneously admitted to the United Nations on September 17.
Kim Il-sung dies and is succeeded by his son Kim Jong-il, beginning a hereditary leadership transition.
North Korea signs the Agreed Framework with the United States, pledging to freeze its plutonium program.
A devastating famine known as the Arduous March kills hundreds of thousands amid economic collapse.
North Korea launches the Taepodong-1 rocket over Japan, alarming the international community.
Kim Jong-il hosts South Korean President Kim Dae-jung in the first inter-Korean summit in Pyongyang.
North Korea withdraws from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty, escalating the nuclear crisis.
North Korea conducts its first underground nuclear test, drawing UN sanctions.
Kim Jong-il dies and his son Kim Jong-un assumes leadership, continuing dynastic rule.
Kim Jong-un orders the execution of his powerful uncle Jang Song-thaek, consolidating his grip on power.
North Korea tests intercontinental ballistic missiles and detonates a powerful nuclear device, prompting tighter sanctions.
Kim Jong-un meets South Korean President Moon Jae-in at the DMZ and US President Donald Trump in Singapore.
North Korea seals its borders against the COVID-19 pandemic, deepening its economic isolation.
North Korea declares itself an irreversible nuclear weapons state and conducts a record number of missile tests.
